transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Hello, Karen. I'm glad you're here today. What feels most important for you to talk about right now?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, I guess I’m not really sure. There’s a lot on my mind, but it’s hard to pinpoint one thing. I’ve been feeling a bit out of place at work lately, like my thoughts don’t really matter. It’s just... I don’t know. Maybe it’s not that important to talk about.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It sounds like you're feeling a bit overwhelmed and unsure about what to focus on. You mentioned feeling out of place at work and that your thoughts don't seem to matter. That sounds really challenging. Can you tell me a little more about what that experience has been like for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Yeah, it’s just... there are times when I want to share my thoughts, especially during discussions about culture and atmosphere at work. But then I think, what’s the point? It feels like my opinions aren’t valued, or maybe they’re just not that important. So, I end up not saying anything. I guess it just makes me feel kind of sad and disconnected from everyone. I don’t know if that makes sense.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: It makes perfect sense, Karen. It sounds like you have thoughts and feelings about the work culture that you want to express, but you’re holding back because you feel your opinions might not be valued. That must create a sense of sadness and disconnection for you. How does it feel when you choose not to share your thoughts in those moments?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: It feels... lonely, I guess. Like I’m just on the outside looking in. I want to be part of the conversation, but then I convince myself that it doesn’t really matter if I speak up or not. So, I just go along with whatever’s happening. But inside, I feel disappointed, like I’m missing out on something important. It’s frustrating, but I don’t know how to change it.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds really tough, Karen. Feeling lonely and disappointed while wanting to be part of the conversation can be quite frustrating. It seems like there’s a conflict between your desire to engage and the belief that your voice doesn’t matter. Can you recall any specific moments when you felt that way, or perhaps times when you did speak up? What happened then?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Um, there have been a few times when I tried to share my thoughts, but it didn’t go well. I remember one meeting where I spoke up about an idea I had, but it felt like no one really listened. They just moved on to the next topic. I felt really embarrassed afterward, like I shouldn’t have said anything at all. So, I guess that just reinforced the idea that my thoughts aren’t important. It makes me hesitate to speak up again. I don’t want to feel that way again.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: That sounds like a really difficult experience, Karen. It’s understandable that feeling ignored in that meeting would make you hesitant to share your thoughts again. It seems like that moment reinforced a belief that your ideas aren’t valued, which is understandably painful. How do you feel about that experience now, looking back on it? And what do you think it might take for you to feel more comfortable sharing your thoughts in the future?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Looking back, I still feel a bit embarrassed and disappointed. It’s like that moment just stuck with me, and I can’t shake off the feeling that I shouldn’t bother trying again. I guess I wish I could feel more confident about my ideas, but it’s hard when I feel like they don’t matter. 

To feel more comfortable sharing, maybe I’d need some encouragement or support from my colleagues, like if someone acknowledged my thoughts or invited me to share more. But I don’t know if that would happen. It’s just... hard to imagine putting myself out there again after that experience.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It’s completely understandable to still feel embarrassed and disappointed about that moment. It sounds like that experience has really impacted your confidence in sharing your ideas. You mentioned that encouragement or support from your colleagues could help you feel more comfortable, which makes a lot of sense. 

It can be tough to envision putting yourself out there again after feeling dismissed. What do you think might help you take small steps toward sharing your thoughts, even if it feels challenging? Are there any particular situations or people that come to mind where you might feel a bit safer to start?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I think starting small could help. Maybe if I could share my thoughts in a smaller group or even just with someone I trust, that might feel less intimidating. There are a couple of colleagues I feel a bit more comfortable with, so maybe I could try talking to them first. 

I guess I could also prepare what I want to say ahead of time, so I feel more confident when the moment comes. But still, there’s that nagging thought in the back of my mind that makes me doubt if it’s worth it. It’s just hard to shake off those feelings of not being valued.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
It sounds like you have some thoughtful ideas about starting small and reaching out to colleagues you trust, which could be a great way to build your confidence. Preparing what you want to say can also help you feel more ready when the moment arises. 

That nagging doubt about whether it’s worth it seems really challenging to deal with, especially when it ties back to feeling undervalued. It’s understandable that those feelings would linger after your experience. How do you think you might address or challenge that doubt when it comes up? What might help you remind yourself that your thoughts do have value?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I think maybe I could try to remind myself that everyone has different perspectives, and mine is just as valid as anyone else’s. It might help to write down some positive feedback I’ve received in the past or moments when I felt appreciated, just to have something to look back on when those doubts creep in. 

I guess I could also try to reframe my thoughts when I feel that doubt, like telling myself that sharing my ideas is a way to contribute, even if they aren’t perfect. It’s just hard to put that into practice when I’m feeling low. I’m not sure how to make that shift consistently.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
Those are really insightful strategies, Karen. Reminding yourself that your perspective is valid and reflecting on positive feedback can be powerful tools to combat those doubts. It’s completely understandable that putting those thoughts into practice consistently can be difficult, especially during low moments. 

It sounds like you’re already aware of the challenges, which is a great first step. What do you think might help you stay connected to those positive reframes when you’re feeling low? Are there any specific practices or routines you could incorporate into your day that might support you in that?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I think having a routine could really help. Maybe I could set aside a few minutes each day to reflect on what went well or jot down any positive feedback I receive. It might be nice to create a little journal for that, so I can look back on it when I’m feeling down. 

Also, I could try to remind myself to take a few deep breaths or do a quick mindfulness exercise when those doubts come up. Just to pause and reset my thoughts. I guess it’s about finding little ways to stay connected to those positive feelings, even when things get tough. But, I don’t know, it still feels like a lot to manage sometimes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
Creating a routine and a journal to reflect on positive experiences sounds like a wonderful idea, Karen. It can be really grounding to have a space to revisit those moments when you’re feeling down. Incorporating mindfulness exercises and deep breathing is also a great way to help reset your thoughts in challenging moments.

It’s understandable to feel that it might be a lot to manage, especially when you’re already feeling overwhelmed. Perhaps starting with just one small practice at a time could make it feel more manageable. Which of these ideas feels like the most approachable first step for you?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I think starting with the journal might be the most approachable. It feels like a simple way to capture positive moments without feeling overwhelming. I could just write a few lines each day, and it wouldn’t take too much time. 

Once I get into that habit, maybe I could add in some mindfulness exercises later. It feels like a good way to ease into it without putting too much pressure on myself. I just hope I can stick with it, you know? It’s hard to stay motivated sometimes.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
Starting with the journal sounds like a great plan, especially since it feels manageable for you. Writing just a few lines each day can be a gentle way to cultivate positivity without adding too much pressure. 

It’s completely normal to have concerns about staying motivated, especially when you’re trying to establish a new habit. What do you think might help you stay motivated to keep up with the journaling? Are there any strategies or reminders you could put in place to support yourself in this process?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
Maybe setting a specific time each day to write in the journal could help. Like, right before I go to bed or first thing in the morning. That way, it becomes part of my routine, and I won’t forget. 

I could also leave the journal somewhere visible, like on my nightstand, to remind me to use it. And maybe I could set a little goal for myself, like writing in it for a week straight, and then reward myself with something small if I stick to it. That might help keep me motivated, I think.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
Those sound like excellent strategies, Karen! Setting a specific time to write in your journal can definitely help make it a consistent part of your routine. Keeping it visible on your nightstand is a smart reminder, too. 

Having a small goal and rewarding yourself for sticking to it can be a great motivator. It sounds like you’re really thinking through how to make this work for you, which is wonderful. How do you feel about starting this new routine? Is there anything else you’d like to explore or discuss before we wrap up today?

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content:
I feel a bit more hopeful about starting the new routine, actually. It seems more doable now that I’ve thought it through. I guess I just need to take it one step at a time and not be too hard on myself if I miss a day or two. 

I think I’ve shared a lot today, and it’s been helpful to talk about these things. I don’t have anything else specific to explore right now, but I appreciate the support. It feels nice to have a plan moving forward. Thank you.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content:
I’m really glad to hear that you’re feeling more hopeful and that our conversation has been helpful for you, Karen. Taking it one step at a time and being gentle with yourself is such an important mindset. 

It sounds like you’ve made some meaningful progress today, and I appreciate you sharing your thoughts and feelings with me. If you ever want to revisit this or explore anything else in the future, I’m here for you. Wishing you the best as you start your journaling routine! Take care.

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much. I really appreciate your understanding and support. It feels good to have a plan and someone to talk to about all of this. I’ll definitely keep in mind what we discussed, and I’ll try to be kind to myself as I start this new routine. Take care, and I look forward to talking again.

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're very welcome, Karen. I'm glad you found our conversation supportive, and I admire your commitment to being kind to yourself as you start this new routine. Remember, it's all about progress, not perfection. I'm here whenever you want to talk again. Take care, and I look forward to hearing how things go for you!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! That means a lot to me. I’ll keep that in mind—progress, not perfection. I appreciate your support, and I’ll definitely reach out to share how things are going. Take care, and have a great day!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: You're very welcome, Karen! I'm glad that resonated with you. I look forward to hearing how things unfold for you. Take care, and have a wonderful day!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you! I appreciate that. I hope you have a wonderful day too. Take care!

transcript.speaker: therapist
transcript.content: Thank you, Karen! I appreciate your kind words. Take care, and I wish you all the best as you move forward. Looking forward to our next conversation!

transcript.speaker: client
transcript.content: Thank you so much! I’m looking forward to our next conversation too. Take care and all the best to you as well!
